entries.text: Hi guys!
I've been scared to film this video for a long time
but I think it's finally time for me to talk about
the real truth of my life here in Korea.
Today I'm not going to talk about the
aesthetic stuff or the highlights in Korea.
I'm going to talk about the real stuff.
It might get emotional, I might cry
so I didn't put on any mascara today.
But if I'm going to keep sharing my life here on YouTube,
I want to do it honestly.
I come from an immigrant family
so my parents they immigrated from Turkey to Switzerland.
That means that I was raised between two cultures.
Turkish culture at home and Swiss culture outside.
I didn't grow up with money
and I had to take a lot of responsibilities early on.
So if I wanted to afford something,
I'd have to work for it.
And I got my first part-time job when I was 15
and I always tried to not be a burden for my family.
I worked hard, I got good grades, I never complained
and I handled my own problems myself ever since I was a kid.
Nobody told me to do that.
That was just my way of being
considerate towards my family.
But of course I was a kid
so it was hard for me.
It's not like it wasn't hard.
So to kind of escape reality,
I would watch anime and I also got into kpop.
And then during high school,
I think it was the last year of high school,
I got into this Korean reality show called Roommate.
Specifically I watched it for Jackson from Got7
because Got7 was kind of my first kpop group that I really loved.
And while watching Roommate, I really fell in love with Korea
and I really wanted to go to Korea.
Like I was a little bit obsessed
and it wasn't really a thing back then to like Korean things.
Only like very few people liked kpop or Korean shows.
And that interest that I got for Korea, it kind of became
a dream that I started holding on to.
And something that really motivated me,
I was like: Okay, I'm gonna graduate high school
and I'm gonna start university.
And then I'm gonna study abroad in Korea.
That was my big goal.
So I worked my --- off.
While studying at university,
I also worked like 40 hours a week at a little cafe.
Actually it was a creperie. We sold crepe.
Like French pancakes basically.
I saved so much money and
then I really studied abroad for like two semesters.
I really loved it in Korea. I had so much fun.
How many did you save?
Oh, you want to know how much money I saved?
Guys, I saved like 10,000 US dollars for this whole thing.
I don't think I used up all the money though.
And back then Korea was cheap.
Anyway, during my exchange studies,
I really fell in love with Korea.
And I also met my beloved partner, boyfriend, SB.
And you know, during my exchange year,
for the first time in my life,
I felt free.
I didn't have to work. I could dress however I wanted.
I could come home whenever I wanted
and I could just breathe.
After my exchange year, of course, I went back to Switzerland
and I finished my degree.
And started preparing to move to Korea.
After my exchange year, I went back to Switzerland, of course.
And I finished my degree and
I started preparing to move to Korea permanently.
Or at least long term.
I'm going to skip the exact HOW I moved here,
the technicalities.
However, I am going to make a separate video about that.
So you can also take notes and maybe it will help you
if you also want to come to Korea.
Anyway, I worked hard again and finally I did it.
I moved to Korea and I am here now.
But living here as an adult,
I mean a real adult, is so different
than living here as a student.
I can't just live off my savings anymore.
I have responsibilities.
I have a career and I have a dream that I'm chasing.
At first, I thought I was just adjusting to adult life.
Which for me happened to be in Korea.
But it wasn't just bills or work stress
that was a lot for me.
It was something deeper that kind of started shifting.
Like the freedom that I had felt
during my exchange year here in Korea.
It slowly turned into this heavy feeling.
When I'm hard time on an emotional level,
I really struggle putting my finger on what the issue is.
But then I feel it physically.
And then my body tells me something's wrong.
You better find out the reason and do something.
I think people think the hardest part about moving abroad
is the language and the culture.
But honestly for me, that was not a big problem.
Because I grew up bilingual
and I learned so many languages in school.
So for me, it's normal to learn a language.
And also because I grew up biculturally as well.
It was also pretty easy for me,
I guess, to adapt to Korean culture.
I don't really care about
cultural differences or language struggles.
Like it doesn't really affect me that much.
But those things are real.
Of course, there are some struggles that I have sometimes.
But for me, the thing that's really, really
the hardest is the...
It's the kind of loneliness that is hard to explain.
Or maybe it's not that hard.
Like I miss my family.
I miss my childhood friends.
I miss my best friends.
Like the friends that I would give my kidney for
without asking twice.
And I don't only miss people.
I also miss the old version of myself
that wasn't constantly working.
Constantly performing and constantly tired.
And constantly striving for an even better future.
I do have people here.
Loving people who support me and care about me.
But I don't have those deep ride-or-die friendships
that I'm used to from back home.
And it's not at all the fault of the people here.
And I'm not blaming anyone.
It's just me.
I'm keeping myself too busy.
And I'm keeping myself too tired.
And I don't make the time
to really build those deep relationships.
And while struggling with this whole loneliness thing and
this missing my family, missing my friends, missing my past self.
Something horrible happened last year.
I did briefly mention it before.
But the beginning of last year
during a quick trip back home to Switzerland.
My childhood best friend,
he lost consciousness the day
before I arrived in Switzerland.
And then a few days later he passed away.
He's a friend that I really really love so much.
And it's been a year and I still carry it.
And whenever I get sad about it,
I tell myself there are people who have lost family members.
There are people who deserve being sad more than I do.
And I feel stupid for like still being sad.
I know that's not how I should feel.
But I feel like "stop making a big deal over it."
"It's been a year."
"Just get over it."
After losing him,
I just felt like something broke inside of me.
And the same day I found out that he passed away.
I had a deadline for a collaboration.
And I sat there in the afternoon
and I filmed the collaboration.
And edited it.
And it's still up on my channel.
My eyes are red and swollen in that video.
I don't know like any normal person would just be like
Hey man I just lost someone.
So let me take a rest and not film this video.
And the brand that I was
working with would have understood.
Anyway after that trip to Switzerland
of course I came back to Korea.
And I just kept working.
But of course the sadness it stayed with me.
I just suppress things like that.
And suppressing your feelings
is not the best coping mechanism.
Because it will come at some point.
And it will be really hard to work through
the emotions if you keep suppressing them.
So on the one year anniversary of my friend's death
I broke.
Like I just cried uncontrollably.
And I just couldn't handle it.
I just didn't know what to do with myself.
And I'm so grateful that SB was there for me.
He's always there for me.
And he helped me do a little tradition for my friend.
We prepared a meal.
We made some Turkish tea.
And we prepared some sunflower seeds.
Because my friend who passed away was also Turkish.
And we did this whole thing.
And in that moment I didn't feel alone anymore.
And it reminded me that even though I feel so disconnected
from all the loving relationships,
family and friends from back home.
I do have love here.
And I have something real here.
So from the outside my life looks so put together.
Especially on my social media.
I also don't really want to worry anyone back home.
So I don't tell them about my problems either.
So to everyone it seems like
I've got everything figured out.
From the outside perspective it looks like
I'm just living my dream life here in Korea.
Of course people don't see the behind the scenes.
Nobody sees all the pressure I put myself under.
And all the mental load of being self-employed.
Far from home.
Constantly working.
And also trying to deal with burnout.
And sometimes I feel quite invisible here in this country.
Like whenever I go somewhere with SB.
People only talk to him.
Even though I speak fluent Korean.
And although both of us are introverted.
I am more extroverted than him.
He agrees on that.
I'm a really strong personality.
So I'm the one making the decisions.
Like when we picked this apartment.
It's my decision.
Like I want this.
I want this place.
So you better talk to me.
But yeah.
I think these types of struggles.
I don't really put them on my social media.
I guess now is the first time
where I really really show myself this vulnerable.
And with all that said.
I think I need to clarify.
I don't hate living in Korea.
I actually love living in Korea.
Regardless of all the things that I'm struggling with.
I of course love the things that Korea is famous for.
Like the safety.
Being able to walk at home at night
and being safe, you know.
Also the convenience.
The aesthetic cafes.
The food.
And the cool language.
And the amazing pop culture.
But the thing I love the most is
the opportunity this country gave me.
I love that I built this life.
And I didn't just move here and stumble into success.
I didn't start with connections.
And just get everything fed to me in a silver spoon.
I worked for everything that I have.
And I carved it out of nothing.
And Korea gave me the space to
actually build something from zero.
And something I'm really proud of.
Right now I'm doing what I love.
I'm in the industry that I always wanted to be.
And that I always dreamed of being in.
And it always seemed too good to be true.
But it is true.
And it is my dream.
So I'm actually living my dream right now.
And I get to share it with people.
With you guys.
And I know my life is not perfect.
But this life is my life.
And it's my life that I built.
It's not a life someone gave to me.
Or a life someone decided for me.
It's mine.
And to me that means a lot.
And it's the biggest achievement.
I think living in Korea really changed me.
I don't think I'm the same person that moved here.
I used to think that strength means
doing everything by yourself.
Not needing anyone, not breaking down,
just pushing through.
Now I think real strength is
letting people in and letting yourself feel things.
And of course crying when you need to,
just like I did today.
And being soft without shame.
I think this country really gave me the space
to meet parts of myself that I didn't really know before
and to work through all these issues.
And I think with all the years
that I've lived here now
and with having SB by my side,
I think I really grew a lot.
I grew so much.
So would I still move to Korea if I knew
about all the struggles that I would have in advance?
Honestly,
yes, I would still move here.
But I don't think I'd move here
for the reasons that I thought.
I came here looking for freedom
but I think what I found is
resilience and also, of course,
I found love.
I didn't come here to find love but I just found it.
I'm lucky like that.
And I still think that moving to Korea
was the best decision I ever made.
I wouldn't have been able to live my dream
and achieve all my goals if I hadn't made
this big scary decision.
And every day that goes by here,
I'm still learning new things about myself
but also about living in Korea
but also just about living as an adult in general.
This is a good point to end this video
on a pretty positive note, I think.
Yeah, I cried and shared some
vulnerable stuff about my life.
I hope that it wasn't too heavy to listen to.
I really appreciate that you guys are here with me and
sitting down and listening to
me talk about my little life.
I'm so grateful.
Thank you for watching this.
If you've ever felt alone, just know you're not alone.
We're all just figuring life out.
Thank you again.
And...
if you like this video, like, comment, subscribe.
That's just not it.
But yeah, thank you so much for being here
and I'll see you again next week, guys.
Bye-bye!
